---
title: "Data Analysis Basics"
---

::: {.topic-badges}
[Novice]{.level-badge .level-novice} · **Data & Analysis** track · ~1 hour, self-paced
:::

[← Back to the Data & Analysis track](data-analysis.qmd){.track-back}

*Ask clear questions of a dataset and get a first, checkable answer with AI.*

## What you'll be able to do

- Ask clear questions of a dataset and get a first answer with AI help
- Have AI generate and explain simple summaries and a chart (R or Python)
- Spot when an AI answer needs checking against the data

## Overview

AI can read a dataset's description and draft the steps or code to answer a question — loading it, summarizing it, making a quick chart. You stay in charge of whether the question is sensible and whether the answer is right.

Start small and verifiable: one dataset, one question, one chart. Confirm a number or two against what you already know before you trust anything.

## Practice activities

```{=html}
<details class="activity"><summary>Activity 1 · Novice — Interview a dataset (Philadelphia crashes)</summary>
```

**Time** ~20 min · **Tools** ChatGPT Edu + a web browser

**Goal.** Get oriented to a real public dataset and pull a first answer you can check.

**Setup.** Open the [Philadelphia Crashes data](https://opendataphilly.org/datasets/crashes/) page and download the [Crash Data 2020–2024 (CSV)](https://hub.arcgis.com/api/v3/datasets/21154a8f1894449f839d2288726e9837_0/downloads/data?format=csv&spatialRefId=3857&where=1%3D1) — PennDOT crash records inside Philadelphia (~50k rows). Open it in Excel/Sheets, or paste the header row + first 5 data rows into ChatGPT Edu. This is **public** data, so it's fine to use.

**Steps.**

1. Copy the column names and first 5 rows into ChatGPT Edu:

   > Here are the columns and first rows of a Philadelphia crash dataset (PennDOT). In a short table, tell me what each column likely means and its data type. Flag any columns you're unsure about.

2. Ask for question ideas:

   > Based on these columns, list five questions this data can answer about pedestrian safety, from simple to harder. For each, name the columns you'd use.

3. Pick one (e.g., "How many crashes involved a pedestrian each year?") and ask for code + steps:

   > Using the pedestrian-count and crash-year fields, write R (tidyverse) **or** Python (pandas) code to count crashes involving at least one pedestrian, by year. Show the code and explain each line. First confirm the exact field names from my column list.

4. Run the code.

**Expected result.** A small table of pedestrian-involved crashes per year (roughly a few thousand per year).

**Check your work.** Spot-check one year yourself — in Excel, count rows where the pedestrian field is greater than 0. It should match the AI's number.

**Common pitfalls.** Field names in the file often differ from what AI guesses — always confirm against the real columns. Missing values may be blank or coded as 0; decide how to treat them before counting.

**Stretch (optional).** Ask for the same count broken out by hour of day, and note when pedestrian crashes peak.

```{=html}
</details>
```

```{=html}
<details class="activity"><summary>Activity 2 · Novice — From question to chart</summary>
```

**Time** ~15 min · **Tools** ChatGPT Edu + R or Python

**Goal.** Turn one question into a clear, defensible chart.

**Setup.** Same crashes CSV from Activity 1.

**Steps.**

1. Ask for a chart:

   > Write R (ggplot2) or Python (matplotlib) code to make a bar chart of pedestrian-involved crashes by year, with axis labels and a title, using this dataset.

2. Run it, then ask it to explain each line in plain language.

3. Change one thing:

   > Now show counts by hour of day instead of year, and shade the three highest hours darker.

**Expected result.** A labeled bar chart; the hour version typically shows an afternoon/evening peak.

**Check your work.** The chart's yearly totals should match your Activity 1 table exactly.

**Common pitfalls.** Don't let AI invent a year that isn't in the data — confirm the year range. Reject any chart with an unlabeled axis.

**Stretch (optional).** Ask AI to add a one-sentence, plain-language caption summarizing the takeaway for a community audience.

```{=html}
</details>
```


## Check your readiness

Answer these, then check — your score suggests whether to dive in or skim the readings first.

<div class="readiness-quiz">
<script type="application/json" class="quiz-data">
{"questions": [{"q": "What's the safest first step before trusting an AI-computed statistic?", "options": ["Run it twice", "Check it against the raw data or a known value", "Ask the AI if it's sure", "Use a bigger model"], "answer": 1, "explain": "Verification against the source is the habit that catches confident errors."}, {"q": "You have a spreadsheet of identifiable patient records. Which tool is appropriate?", "options": ["A Drexel-approved tool cleared for that data level", "Any chatbot you trust", "NotebookLM", "A personal ChatGPT account"], "answer": 0, "explain": "Match the tool to Drexel's data classification; identifiable records need an approved tool."}, {"q": "Why start with one question and one chart?", "options": ["It's faster to verify and learn from", "Charts are required", "AI can only do one thing", "It saves tokens"], "answer": 0, "explain": "Small, verifiable steps build judgment and catch errors early."}]}
</script>
</div>

## Recommended readings

Available in the shared OneDrive folder **Staff Faculty AI Workshop → Readings**, and online where linked:

- [**AI Agents for Data Scientists: Automations vs Agents**](https://datascienceweekly.substack.com/p/ai-agents-for-data-scientists-automations) — when AI should assist vs. when to keep manual control.
- [**How to Do Agentic Data Science (Eric J. Ma)**](https://ericmjl.github.io/blog/2026/2/1/how-to-do-agentic-data-science/) — a practical workflow for AI-assisted analysis.
- [**Mastering the Data Science Human Skills AI Can't Touch**](https://aitransformer.online/data-science-human-skills-ai-cant-touch/) — the judgment you still own.

## Useful resources

- [ChatGPT Edu (Drexel AI Tools)](https://drexel.edu/provost/ai/tools) — the approved tool for this work, with data rules.
- [Posit / RStudio](https://posit.co) — the standard environment for R analysis.
- [tidyverse](https://www.tidyverse.org) — R packages for reading, summarizing, and plotting data.

::: {.callout-warning}
## Data rules
Use **public or non-sensitive** data for practice; never paste identifiable records into an unapproved tool. See [Tools & Access](../tools.qmd) and Drexel's [AI Tools page](https://drexel.edu/provost/ai/tools).
:::
